facts.text: The Inca Empire was the largest empire in pre-Columbian America.
Its heartland lay in the Andes Mountains, with Cusco serving as the imperial capital.
The Incas built an enormous road network stretching thousands of miles across difficult mountain terrain.
Machu Picchu, one of the most famous Inca sites, was built in the 15th century high in the Andes.
The Inca ruler was known as the Sapa Inca and was treated as a sacred monarch.
Rather than a conventional alphabet, the Incas used quipu — knotted cords — for record keeping and administration.
Terrace farming allowed the Incas to grow crops efficiently on steep mountain slopes.
Llamas and alpacas were essential for transport, wool, meat, and daily life in the Andes.
The empire was divided into regions and held together through roads, storehouses, labor systems, and imperial administration.
Inca stonework is famous for its precision, with blocks fitted so tightly that mortar was often unnecessary.
Atahualpa was the emperor captured by Francisco Pizarro during the Spanish conquest in the 1530s.
Civil war between Atahualpa and Huáscar weakened the empire just before the Spanish invasion.
Although the empire fell to conquest, Inca influence remains strong in the languages, traditions, and communities of the Andes today.
The Inca Empire became one of the largest and most remarkable states in pre-Columbian America.
Its reach across the Andes made it one of the great civilizations of world history.
Inca Empire is one of the standout topics people often explore within Incas.
